preamble: The voiced dental fricative /ð/ can undergo a phonetic transformation to the alveolar nasal /n/, primarily through processes of assimilation in connected speech. This shift involves a change in the manner of articulation from a fricative to a nasal, and a slight adjustment in the place of articulation from dental to alveolar.

examples.note: Assimilation of /ð/ to /n/ when between a preceding /n/ and a vowel.
Progressive manner assimilation of /ð/ to a preceding /n/ in rapid speech, as in 'wasn't there'.
Progressive manner assimilation of /ð/ to a preceding /n/ in rapid speech, as in 'win the race'.
A broader change of initial /ð/ to [n] in some phonological contexts, as in 'than'.

sources: Jones, J. (2023). African American English intensifier dennamug: Using twitter to investigate syntactic change in low-frequency forms. Frontiers in Communication, 8.
